data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0943 ET - U.S. President Trump's tightening of immigration controls could present a headwind to digital assets, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. "For tech investors and digital asset markets--where the free movement of global talent is core--such moves might presage constraints in the future, impacting innovation cycles and digital asset flows." That comes amid Trump's expanding foray into cryptocurrencies, including the establishment of a strategic bitcoin reserve. Trump's efforts have improved bitcoin's mainstream adoption but also bring ethical and regulatory issues, he says. Bitcoin last trades down 0.6% at $105,578 after hitting a 10-day low of $104,648 earlier, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0859 ET - Zscaler's latest quarterly report, in which it topped analyst estimates and raised guidance, represents another breakout moment for the cybersecurity stalwart amid a customer shift toward cloud-based security solutions, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. Zscaler is seeing strong demand for its zero trust security platform and its AI security solutions, which will be well received by Wall Street in a choppy spending environment, the analysts say. The company is scoring new clients and reeling in more customers that provide $1 million or more in annual recurring revenue, they say, maintaining an outperform rating on the stock and moving their price target up to $300 from $240. Shares rise 4.9% to $263.50. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0818 ET - Marvell Technology's lack of data center upside in 1Q and its 2Q guidance leaves more questions about the current ramp of the artificial intelligence business, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The chip maker logged adjusted EPS of 62 cents and revenue of $1.9 billion in 1Q, compared to expectations of 61 cents a share in adjusted EPS and revenue of $1.88 billion. Marvell guided for adjusted EPS between 62 cents and 72 cents and sales of $2 billion, while the Street expected 66 cents a share in adjusted EPS and sales of $1.98 billion. "In short, without data center and AI upside in the year, the stock is a 'show-me story' and may have trouble breaking out," the analysts say. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0806 ET - Marvell Technology's latest results may have fallen short of some investors' lofty expectations but the company still has plenty of tailwinds, Benchmark's Cody Acree says in a research note. The chip maker's modest upside results and guidance compared to the standout performance by Nvidia is weighing on its stock. But Acree notes that on a comparative basis, the two companies are seeing similar trends with broad based demand strength in AI driving data center growth in both compute and networking. While Acree understands investor's caution, he's encouraged by a few factors including Marvell's market leading interconnect portfolio and the broad cyclical recovery it's seeing in both its carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking business, which he expects to be a positive catalyst for sentiment and its shares. Shares decline 3.4% to $61.59 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0556 ET - Xiaomi's strong momentum across all business segments is likely to continue this year, Bernstein analysts write in a note. The company's 1Q performance was impressive across the board, thanks to significant gains in smartphone market share in China and robust growth in Internet-of-Things demand, they say. Xiaomi is well-positioned to benefit from China's subsidy policies in both the smartphone and IoT sectors due to its product portfolio, they say. Looking ahead, the brokerage raised its revenue forecast by 4% for this year and 3% for 2026. Bernstein reiterates the outperform rating for the company and raises its target price to HK$60.00 from HK$55.00. Shares last closed at HK$50.95. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0358 ET - Kuaishou Technology's outlook looks positive in 2Q with steady core businesses and encouraging AI progress, according to Deutsche Bank Research analyst Leo Chiang in commentary. Kling AI, a tool for creating imaginative images and videos, continued to gain traction, generating 150 million yuan in revenue in the first quarter, Chiang says. This progress is likely to continue, he adds. The analyst expects its core ads and e-commerce businesses to maintain steady growth in 2Q as it continues to enhance its video-content ecosystem and monetization capabilities. DB keeps a buy rating, with a target price of HK$75.00. Shares were last traded at HK$53.00. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2400 ET - AI could continue to drive long-term equity performance despite near-term volatility, UBS global wealth management chief investment office says in a research note. UBS expects global AI spending to reach $360 billion in 2025, up 60%, and further grow to $480 billion in 2026. Recent results from the four leading global cloud platforms showed a solid 24% on-year growth in combined revenue, they note, adding that robust cloud growth should narrow the gap between AI spending and monetization, which is also supported by rising AI adoption rates across industries and company sizes. Despite being bullish on the long-term trend, UBS favors a more balanced exposure between semiconductor and software names in the near term to navigate volatility. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2351 ET - Superloop retains its status as Morgans analysts' pick of Australian telecom stocks, helped by an ungeared balance sheet that supports a range of options. Morgans analysts tell clients in a note that the stock is close to fair value based on current earnings, but say they see potential upside from strong sales momentum and the balance-sheet options. They note that telecom companies typically can handle net debt of one or two times Ebitda, which indicates Superloop could return capital to shareholders or undertake acquisitions, in which they see the latter as more likely. Morgans raises its target price by 15% to A$3.00 and keeps an add rating on the stock, which is down 1.1% at A$2.67. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1415 ET - ATS' backlog is at a record high and should support revenue growth and some margin growth in fiscal 2026. TD Cowen's Cherilyn Radbourne says in a report that ATS has a backlog of C$2 billion, up 19% year over year. She notes that the backlog in the highly regulated life sciences market declined marginally quarter-on-quarter, but is up 38% year-over-year and remains by far the largest backlog contributor at 56%. ATS set new backlog records in consumer products, food & beverage, and nuclear, which reduced EVs to 10% from 12% in F3Q. Meanwhile, tariffs shouldn't be an issue either. ATS is monitoring tariff-related economic uncertainty, but hasn't seen material impact on bookings to-date, she adds. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1302 ET - Mortgage rates rose to 6.89% this week, posing significant challenges for homebuyers and keeping the "lock-in effect" in place, says Realtor.com senior economist Jake Krimmel. According to Freddie Mac the 30-year mortgage rate rose 3 basis points to its highest level since Feb. 6. "With rates threatening to cross 7% for the first time since January," Krimmel says, "the 'lock-in effect'--where current homeowners are reluctant to sell and give up their lower mortgage rates--shows no signs of easing up." According to a recent Realtor.com survey, just 2% of respondents said they would be willing to buy with rates above 6%. But new listings and total homes for sale continued to rise last month, and homes are staying on the market for longer. Buyers may be regaining some bargaining power despite rising interest rates. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0947 ET - The consumer-health industry could see more merger-and-acquisition activity, but smaller deals for certain brands or segments seem more likely than large-scale takeovers, Citi analysts say in a research note. Recent spinoffs such as Kenvue and Haleon, potential splits or asset reviews at Bayer and Reckitt Benckiser, and acquisition attempts like Unilever's for Haleon in 2022 left investors wondering whether the industry could consolidate, Citi says. Healthy balance sheets and interest from the likes of Procter & Gamble suggest there is room for acquisitions, but antitrust concerns and diverse portfolios make large deals unlikely, Citi says. In a large takeover, the buyer would probably need a significant portfolio reshaping afterward, the analysts say. The Stoxx Europe 600 Personal & Household Goods index is up 0.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0858 ET - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0455 ET - Sanofi shares fall in European morning trading, while Regeneron Pharmaceuticals is sharply lower in U.S. premarket trading, after the companies reported mixed results from two late-stage trials of a respiratory drug they are jointly developing. Sanofi and Regeneron said drug candidate Itepekimab missed the key goal of a phase 3 trial, but met the primary objective in the other study. Paris-listed shares in Sanofi fall 5.8%, while Regeneron shares drop 12% in U.S. premarket trading. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0232 ET - News that Swiss eyecare specialist Alcon received its first U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval since its listing in 2019 is slightly positive, but comes as little surprise, Vontobel's Sibylle Bischofberger says in a research note. The drug, Tryptyr, was approved to treat the signs and symptoms of dry eye disease, a condition that affects around 38 million people in the U.S. and for which there is a need for new treatment options, the analyst says. "We expect Alcon to continue performing well, especially with the launch of several products in 2025. However, results may remain somewhat volatile due to challenging market conditions," Vontobel says. Zurich-listed shares closed 1.95% lower Wednesday at 70.56 Swiss francs, with markets in Switzerland closed Thursday due to a holiday. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0135 ET - Bangkok Dusit Medical Services' earnings outlook appears softer, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. Management has guided for 2025 revenue growth of 4%-6%, down from its earlier 7%-8% growth target for 1H 2025, which probably reflects a weaker economic outlook for Thailand, the analyst says. Management also noted Middle East revenue may decline in the long term as countries in the region invest in their own healthcare systems, the analyst says. The brokerage reduces its 2025-2027 earnings estimates for the Thai hospital operator by 2%-5%. It lowers the stock's target price to THB27.50 from THB31.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are unchanged at THB21.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2317 ET - IHH Healthcare is poised for long-term growth, backed by solid execution, a strong regional presence, and resilient demand for premium healthcare, says RHB IB analyst Oong Chun Sung in a note. He is upbeat on IHH's organic and inorganic growth strategies, especially after Malaysia scrapped plans for a diagnosis-related group payment system, which removes a key regulatory overhang. DRG is a system that groups hospital cases by diagnosis and treatment, with each group reimbursed at a fixed rate regardless of actual treatment cost. Oong raises IHH 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 7%, 11% and 11%, respectively, after 1Q earnings beat expectation. RHB raises IHH's target price to MYR9.00 from MYR8.70, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.7% lower at MYR6.86.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2218 ET - Bangkok Chain Hospital's tone seems positive on the recent high budget spending by Thailand's Social Security Office, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report, as they maintain a buy rating on the stock. Management clarified that this spending won't affect the current "AdjRW>2" rate of THB12,000 per patient, easing investors' concerns over the past few days, the analysts say. SSO's payments to flood victims in September 2024 and earthquake victims in March 2025 raised concerns that the SSO may lack funds to maintain the current rate, they note. However, the brokerage lowers the stock's target price to THB17.50 from THB20.00, based on its 2025 enterprise value/Ebitda multiple of 14.0x. Shares last closed at THB13.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2139 ET - Aroa Biosurgery's conservative revenue guidance implies that the wound-treatment company's elevated growth momentum should continue, according to Wilsons analysts. They write in a note that the guidance is seen as achievable by the market, adding that they anticipate a potentially significant increase in productivity from its Myriad product sales reps in fiscal 2026. They reckon that the stock should trade in line with wound-care peers at three times 2026 revenue. Wilsons lifts its target price 9.3% to A$0.82 and stays overweight on the stock, which is flat at A$0.53. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2105 ET - IHH Healthcare is poised for continued growth, supported by a planned 28% increase in hospital bed capacity by 2028, mainly in Malaysia and India, Hong Leong IB analyst Chee Kok Siang says in a note. In Singapore and Hong Kong, IHH plans to expand across the care continuum with a focus on ambulatory and primary care, while also aiming to turn around its China operations, he says. Ongoing refurbishments at Singapore's Mount Elizabeth Hospital at Orchard may pressure margins until 2Q, he adds. Management remains open to merger-and-acquisition opportunities, targeting value-accretive assets in Malaysia, India, Turkey, and Europe, he notes. Hong Leong raises IHH's target price to MYR9.06 from MYR9.03 while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.7% lower at MYR6.86.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2050 ET - If Ryman Healthcare has a vision for the next three to five years that goes beyond modest improvements in cash generation then management doesn't appear ready to share it with the market, says Forsyth Barr. It had hoped Ryman's FY 2025 result would offer clarity on the path ahead, stating it's "a raison d'etre to own Ryman through what is undoubtedly going to be a prolonged turnaround." Instead, Ryman's FY 2026 guidance disappointed. Ryman expects 1,200 total sales at the midpoint of its guidance range, substantially below Forsyth Barr's estimate of 1,350 ahead of its result. "The lower sales expectation is likely the core driver of Ryman's unwillingness to guide to positive free cash flow for FY 2026--another disappointment," analyst Aaron Ibbotson says. Forsyth Barr retains a neutral call on Ryman. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0951 ET - Investor demand for significant risk transfer deals remains strong and European banks are making the most of the opportunity, S&P Global Ratings says in a report. SRTs are a capital and risk management tool through which credit losses on reference loan portfolios are transferred to third-party investors. "The region's largest lenders dominate transaction activity, but issuance is becoming broader based as the growing investor base and favorable pricing attract more banks to the market," credit analyst Richard Barnes says. Spain's Santander has dethroned Barclays as Europe's most active SRT originator, according to S&P. Last year saw an increase in BBVA, Erste Group and NatWest's activity while AIB Group and DNB completed their first transactions of this kind. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0914 ET - Julius Baer needs to show that growth will be restored and that its flow rate trajectory will increase to lift its share price, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note ahead of the Swiss private bank's investor day on Tuesday. "We expect it to be a relatively tame affair as the group displays its new disciplined approach to risk, culture, and profitability," analysts write, noting that investor confidence is low due to a series of credit missteps. The move to streamline operations under the leadership of the new CEO since the start of the year has been a helpful first step, the analysts say. However, demonstrating strong sustainable growth is arguably a more powerful driver, they add. Shares have fallen 7.8% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0312 ET - M&G's partnership with Japanese life insurance company Dai-ichi Life is very positive news for the U.K. investment management firm and will boost its shares, J.P. Morgan analysts say. The deal is a large strategic commitment from Japan's largest listed life insurer, they note. Dai-ichi Life's decision to acquire a significant stake in M&G shows a high level of confidence in the execution of the London-listed company's strategy. The two partners could also develop new products together, the analysts add. Shares in M&G are up 8% at 2.42 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

2044 ET - A shift in deposit mix looks like being a stronger margin headwind for Westpac than for National Australia Bank, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They point to Westpac's larger exposure to retail banking and the pricing of its retail savings accounts as justification for this view. The MS analysts observe in a note to clients that the proportion of savings in term deposits remains lower than at the end of the last decade, and that customers are still shifting toward higher-cost deposits such as those offering bonus enticements. This is one of the reasons that MS prefers NAB over Westpac among Australian bank stocks. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1302 ET - Mortgage rates rose to 6.89% this week, posing significant challenges for homebuyers and keeping the "lock-in effect" in place, says Realtor.com senior economist Jake Krimmel. According to Freddie Mac the 30-year mortgage rate rose 3 basis points to its highest level since Feb. 6. "With rates threatening to cross 7% for the first time since January," Krimmel says, "the 'lock-in effect'--where current homeowners are reluctant to sell and give up their lower mortgage rates--shows no signs of easing up." According to a recent Realtor.com survey, just 2% of respondents said they would be willing to buy with rates above 6%. But new listings and total homes for sale continued to rise last month, and homes are staying on the market for longer. Buyers may be regaining some bargaining power despite rising interest rates. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0915 ET - Oil and gas will be needed as energy sources and for the manufacturing of essential products for decades to come, Repsol's Chairman Antonio Brufau said Friday. Europe lacks a strategic plan for the transition away from refined fossil fuels, he said. "More than 95% of passenger and freight transport in Europe is powered by liquid fuels produced in refineries," he added. Brufau called for a reduced tax regime for renewable fuels, saying it would facilitate investment and boost consumption. Shares trade up 0.9% at 11.9 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0908 ET - Repsol's chairman is calling on Europe to use pragmatism when developing energy transition policies in order to avoid harming competitiveness. Speaking at the Spanish oil company's annual general meeting, Antonio Brufau says that Europe needs to create a true energy union, and that it currently relies too much on Russia and China for energy. It makes no sense for Europe to pursue decarbonization strategies in isolation from others, especially when its carbon-dioxide emissions are significantly lower than China and the U.S., he says. Repsol will continue to invest oil and natural gas, as well as new renewable fuels, he adds. Shares trade up 0.7% at 11.87 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0905 ET - Oil futures are lower with the market widely expecting OPEC+ to raise its output targets by another 411,000 barrels in July. The bearish factors that have driven the selloff this year, including tariffs and OPEC+ supply, may be mostly priced in by now, analysts at Citi say in a note. Oil has found support in "geopolitical risks vis-à-vis Iran and Russia-Ukraine, still-to-be-replenished low inventories, and firm refined product demand and cracks." The lack of significant increase in OPEC+ exports despite the accelerated unwinding of cuts has bolstered the view that the market might not turn as loose as previously expected, while Middle Eastern countries are building crude inventory for increased fuel use during the summer cooling season, they say. WTI is down 1.1% at $60.29, and Brent for August delivery is down 1.2% at $62.59 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

2228 ET - Tenaga Nasional is set to benefit from record electricity demand driven by data centers, commercial growth and hot weather, Affin Hwang IB analyst Isaac Chow says in a note. He expects electricity sales to rebound strongly in 2Q amid rising peak demand. Tenaga has completed 21 data-center projects, and 17 are under construction, he notes. Management also sees continued strong interest from data-center developers and aims to secure an additional 10 electricity supply agreements, each with a maximum demand of 150 MW to 200 MW by end-2025, he adds. Chow cuts Tenaga's 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 4%-8% amid a higher tax rate. Affin Hwang lowers the target price to MYR16.20 from MYR16.70, with a buy rating. Shares fall 1.0% to MYR13.94. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2134 ET - China Aviation Oil (Singapore) may benefit from some tailwinds, Phillip Securities Research's Liu Miaomiao says in a report. Profit from its associates is expected to grow at least 12% in 2025, supported by continued rebound of inbound leisure and business travel, the analyst says. Also, the company's volume in trading of jet fuel and other oil products is likely to pick up in 1H, driven by rising demand for jet fuel and sustainable aviation fuel, the analyst says. The brokerage upgrades the stock's rating to accumulate from neutral and raises the target price to S$0.90 from S$0.85 to reflect recent share-price performance and improving financial metrics. Shares last closed at S$0.83. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1848 ET - Forsyth Barr worries Infratil's operating cash flow could worsen if it sells certain assets to realize some NZ$1 billion of proceeds. Infratil signaled the goal this week, stating that businesses unlikely to scale under its ownership are candidates for sale. Analyst Aaron Ibbotson thinks Diagnostic Imaging, Wellington International Airport, a 10% stake in Contact Energy, and RetireAustralia fit that description. "However, outside of RA, those three businesses provide the lion's share of operating cash flow," Forsyth Barr says. Infratil suggests its CDC data centers and Longroad Energy businesses could pick up the tab, but Forsyth Barr says that is many years away. "We applaud the clarified focus on having a sustainable operating-cash-flow position, but short of a dividend reduction we do not see a clear path," the bank says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1512 ET - The boost to oil from a U.S. court ruling against President Trump's trade tariffs proves short-lived, and futures turn lower with OPEC+ supply concerns remaining as a headwind. Another 411,000-barrel increase in July would bring accumulated unwinding of output cuts to 1.37 million b/d, although "the actual supply boost may be significantly less than projected," StoneX's Alex Hodes says in a note. U.S. inventory data, including a 2.8 million barrel crude stock draw, largely on higher exports, did little for prices. Gasoline stocks fell more than expected as demand picked up to almost 9.5 million b/d ahead of the summer driving season. WTI settles down 1.5% at $60.94 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.2% to $64.15. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1538 GMT - End markets are too soft for BRP to see a good runway to growth. CIBC's Mark Petrie says in a report that the leisurecraft maker remains hurt by excess industry inventory and cautious consumer demand that skews away from its strengths. While management is trying to turn things around with new products and innovation, "we ultimately see a broader demand recovery as the key to more normalized margins and a healthier valuation, and believe this will take time." Meanwhile, tariffs remain a headwind, but these are easing, Petrie says. Still, looking at the broader market, peer inventory levels remain elevated, and non-current units continue to outperform because of the elevated discount programs in place. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways' partnership has a lot to like, TD Cowen's Tom Fitzgerald and Helane Becker say in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. "Miles in JetBlue's loyalty program become a more relevant currency with United's network while United gets a beachhead in JFK and increased access to Boston and Florida," say the analysts. They view this as a negative for both Delta Air Lines and American Airlines, but more so the latter given it was already challenged in the Northeast corridor. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1358 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways both benefit from their partnership given the complementary nature of their respective footprints, UBS' Thomas Wadewitz says in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. The analyst says the biggest positive for JetBlue would be getting access to United's vast network, which could make its loyalty program more attractive as its customers could potentially use points from the program to fly United to markets not currently served by JetBlue. United gets a lift from its presence in JFK with seven round-trip slots beginning 2027, plus other hubs it doesn't currently have a strong footprint, adds Wadewitz. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1329 GMT - BRP's inventory headwinds are easing, opening the doors to growth and expansion. TD Cowen's Brian Morrison says that the leisurecraft manufacturer's inventory getting back on track will provide a path to revenue growth and margin expansion, and ultimately support market-share gains. Lower volumes could help boost margin, coupled with lower promotional intensity and product mix. TD upgrades its recommendation to buy from hold, even though there is still a cautious outlook on near-term consumer demand. "The cadence of recovery remains uncertain, but we have visibility to trough earnings that no longer warrants a trough multiple," Morrison says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Li Auto looks set to regain positive sales momentum on product refreshes, Bernstein analysts write in a note. Although the company's 1Q results were largely in line with expectations, it provided positive 2Q delivery guidance of 123,000-128,000 units, implying sales of between 45,000 and 47,000 units each in May and June, they add. The revamped Li Mega has a strong order backlog, and production capacity is being ramped up to achieve monthly deliveries of 2,500-3,000 units by July, the analysts say. They also note that Li Auto is receiving more than 10,000 new orders weekly for the updated full-electric MPV and is confident it can return to monthly deliveries of 50,000 units soon. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0938 GMT - Li Auto's 2025 total sales remain challenging, Nomura analysts write in a note. The company's first-quarter earnings were largely in line with expectations and it and guided for 123,000-128,000 units of total shipments for the second quarter. With Li Auto's L series updates and efforts on promotions, the company should be able to maintain existing sales volumes despite an increase in the number of competitive new models in the market, they say. However, since the company doesn't have a plan for new hybrid plug-in models this year, the brokerage remains cautious on Li Auto's 2025 shipment growth. If the new full-electric SUVs are welcomed by the market, that could be a major growth opportunity for the company by the end of this year, Nomura says.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0718 GMT - Bajaj Auto's business focus is likely to offer sufficient operational levers to sustain margins despite rising input costs, Axis Securities' Shridhar Kallani says in a research report. This focus includes expansion of its portfolio of premium motorcycles, electric two-wheelers and electric three-wheelers, and leveraging of its new product launches, the analyst notes. For FY 2026, management is seeking to gain leadership in the 125cc+ motorcycle segment, scale key two- and three-wheeler platforms and increase exports, particularly in Latin America, the analyst adds. The brokerage raises the stock's target price to INR9,890.00 from INR9,380.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 2.5% lower at INR8,652.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0647 GMT - SIA Engineering stands to benefit from new comprehensive services agreements signed with Singapore Airlines and Scoot recently, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a research report. Based on the maintenance, repair, and overhaul services provider's announcement on the Singapore Exchange, the agreements are expected to generate a total labor revenue of S$1.3 billion over two years. This translates to a significant labor revenue step-up of 54.7% per annum when compared to the previous agreements, the analyst estimates. OCBC updates its model to incorporate the new contracts, and raises the stock's fair value estimate to S$3.00 from S$2.64 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 0.4% lower at S$2.71. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0607 GMT - Samvardhana Motherson International may benefit from its entry into new segments, Nomura analysts say in a research report, as they raise the stock's target price to INR170.00 from INR155.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Management has commented that the automotive component supplier will target new solutions like components for semiconductor machines and printed circuit board assemblies for autos, the analysts note. Management also said the Indian company will seek to continue ramping up production of consumer electronics. The brokerage raises its FY 2026-2027 revenue estimates for the company by 3%-5% to reflect a stronger EUR/INR, partly offset by lower organic growth assumptions. Shares are 1.7% higher at INR154.75. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0509 GMT - Somboon Advance Technology faces an earnings hit from Thailand's weak economy, which is likely to continue weighing on the auto industry, Thanachart Securities' Rata Limsuthiwanpoom says in a research report. Thailand's auto production fell 12% on year during first four months of 2025, with the auto sector likely to stay under pressure from a global slowdown driven by U.S. tariffs, the analyst says. The brokerage cuts its 2025-2027 earnings forecasts for the Thai auto-parts manufacturer by 21%-40%, and lowers the stock's target price to THB14.00 from THB16.00. However, the brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating on the valuation and dividend yield angles. Shares are 1.6% higher at THB12.40. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0043 GMT - Mainfreight's earnings cycle appears to have troughed to Forsyth Barr. The logistics group's FY 2025 was mixed with a strong performance in Australia more than offset by profit declines in New Zealand, the Americas and Asia. Analyst Andy Bowley sees scope for Mainfreight's earnings to accelerate over the next 18 months, returning to trend growth in FY 2027. Still, Forsyth Barr warns this recovery is likely to be non-linear. "Uncertainty persists, particularly around tariffs, with a volume slowdown through May offset by strong forward bookings for June/July and accompanied by volatility in rates," Forsyth Barr says. It notes that Mainfreight is reluctant to talk about "green shoots" in New Zealand just yet. The bank retains a neutral call on Mainfreight. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: U.S. trade deficit in goods plummets in April, pointing to stronger second-quarter GDP
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Imports drop a record $19.8 billion as tariffs bite

The numbers: The U.S. goods deficit narrowed very sharply in April as foreign orders ground to a halt after President Donald Trump announced sweeping, higher tariffs.

The trade deficit in goods narrowed by 46% to $87.6 billion in April from a record $162.3 billion in March, according to the Commerce Department's advanced estimate released Friday. That's the narrowest trade gap since December 2023.

After stockpiling consumer goods in the first quarter to avoid the then-anticipated tariffs, businesses essentially stopped importing goods in April.

The deficit was much narrower than the $143 billion deficit forecast by economists, according to an Econoday Inc. survey.

Falling imports will likely boost GDP growth in the second quarter.

Key details: Imports dropped a record 19.8% in April after a 5.7% jump in March.

Oliver Allen, senior economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics, said he suspected that drops in precious metals, pharmaceuticals and computer equipment led the decline.

Exports were up 3.4% in April after a 2.3% rise in the prior month.

Also in the report, wholesale inventories were unchanged in May after a 0.4% rise in the prior month. Nonautomotive retail inventories rose 0.3% after a 1% rise in March.

Big picture: Companies moved quickly early this year to bring product into the country ahead of the imposition of new tariffs on goods being imported to the U.S.

Economists said that it was too soon to know for sure but that the data improved the prospect of a positive second-quarter GDP reading.

There may be a jump in imports in June following the temporary bilateral truce between the U.S. and China announced in the middle of May, said Omair Sharif, founder of Inflation Insights. In addition, companies will draw down inventories that they had built up in the first quarter, and this will be a drag on growth, he said.

Looking ahead: "If imports dropped off and inventories did not fall to math, the GDP growth will be stronger. However, we will be surprised if stockpiles of goods do not drop hard before the second quarter is over," said Carl Weinberg, chief economist at High Frequency Economics.

Market reaction: Stocks SPX were mixed in Friday morning trading while the 10-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y was down slightly to 4.221% in early trading.

-Greg Robb

At the Annual General Meeting of Kaldalón hf., held on 3 April 2025, the shareholders approved the Board of Directors’ proposal to reduce the company’s share capital. The reduction amounts to ISK 337,501,390 or 33,750,139 shares, whereas each share has a nominal value of ten (10) ISK, and pertains to treasury shares acquired by the company under its share buyback programmes. 

Following the reduction, the company’s total share capital will amount to ISK 10,858,183,440. The share capital is divided into 1,085,818,344 shares of ISK ten (10) in nominal value.

A request has been submitted to Nasdaq Iceland and Nasdaq CSD, and the reduction will be executed on Monday, 2 June 2025.

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data, Likely ECB Rate Cut in Focus
data.description:
Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting June 2.

U.S. jobs data for May will be the focus of the week ahead as investors look to see the extent to which tariffs are affecting jobs and what that might mean for interest rates, alongside ISM data on U.S. manufacturing and services sector activity.

A decision by the European Central Bank will also be closely watched, where a rate cut is widely expected. The Bank of Canada also announces its latest policy decision and is expected to leave rates unchanged.

In Asia, the Reserve Bank of India will be in the spotlight with its upcoming interest-rate decision. Manufacturing purchasing managers' surveys and inflation data across the region will also be closely watched.

U.S.

U.S. monthly jobs data for May on Friday will be key for investors as they assess the impact of U.S. tariffs on the jobs market. Any signs of weakness could increase the chances of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates sooner rather than later.

"With so many soft indicators pointing to a slump, modest confirmation of a slowdown from hard data should be enough to put [rate] cuts on the table," Standard Chartered's global head of G-10 FX research Steve Englander said in a note.

U.S. money markets currently price in around two interest-rate cuts this year, with a first reduction not expected until October, LSEG data show.

Other signs on the health of the jobs market will be provided ahead of Friday's release, including JOLTS job openings figures for April on Tuesday, ADP private payrolls figures for May on Wednesday and weekly jobless claims on Thursday.

Another key set of data for determining how tariff uncertainty has affected business activity and confidence will be the latest ISM data on manufacturing and services sector activity in May. ISM manufacturing data are due Monday and services data Wednesday.

Other data include trade data for April and first-quarter revised productivity and costs figures on Thursday.

CANADA

The Bank of Canada announces an interest rate decision on Wednesday. Most in the market expect it to leave its key policy rate unchanged at 2.75% due to high inflation. However, a 25 basis-point rate cut is possible if the central bank chooses to focus on the strains facing Canada's economy.

Canadian trade data on Thursday and labor-market data on Friday will provide insight into how the economy is faring in light of President Trump's tariffs.

EUROZONE

The European Central Bank's monetary policy meeting will be the week's highlight. Markets fully price in a 25-basis-point interest-rate cut to lower the deposit rate to 2%, a move that quarterly staff forecasts on GDP and inflation could underpin.

"With regard to the future monetary policy course, the monetary authorities are likely to keep a low profile and refer to the data-dependent nature of monetary policy," DZ Bank analyst Christian Reicherter said in a note. "However, in order to be prepared for potential downside risks, the ECB will keep the door open for a further easing step," he said. This, however, should not take place before the summer break, in his view.

ABN Amro analysts expect a downgrade to 2025 growth and inflation forecasts, which should pave the way for further rate cuts over the summer.

Similarly, flash estimated eurozone inflation data for May on Tuesday can give the ECB the justification to go ahead with easing.

"HICP flash inflation for May is likely to show a renewed decline, with the headline moving lower on the back of falling petrol prices, while core inflation should also move lower as the Easter boost to services inflation unwinds," ABN Amro analysts said in a note.

Also on Tuesday, eurozone unemployment data for April will be released.

French, German and eurozone final manufacturing PMI data for May are due Monday, and final services PMI on Wednesday.

German manufacturing orders for April are due on Thursday, while German and French industrial production figures are up for release on Friday.

Slovakia will launch a new 2029-dated bond on Monday, Germany taps 2027-dated Schatz on Tuesday, while Spanish and French auctions are scheduled for Thursday.

U.K.

The Bank of England Money and Credit data is set to be released on Monday. The data is expected to show a modest decline in mortgage approvals, economists at Investec Economics said in a note. The BOE Monetary & Financial Statistics data is also due on Monday.

The final reading of the U.K. May purchasing managers' survey for manufacturing will be released Monday, followed by the final services PMI reading on Wednesday.

The Debt Management Office plans to sell October 2063 gilt Tuesday and March 2028 gilt Wednesday.

POLAND

Poland's central bank announces an interest-rate decision on Wednesday and is expected to leave its key policy rate unchanged at 5.25% after cutting the rate by 25 basis points in May.

Analysts expect that the central bank could cut interest rates by July.

"It is likely that by July the NBP [Poland's central bank] inflation projection may reflect a lower energy price path, which should lower the central bank's forecast and thus open doors for additional monetary easing," Citi analysts said in a note.

SCANDINAVIA

Swedish flash inflation data for May are due on Thursday. Inflation fell to an annual rate of 0.3% in April, marking the lowest reading since November 2020 and well below the Riksbank's 2% target. At the Riksbank's policy meeting in early May, it said it was "somewhat more probable that inflation will be lower than it will be higher" compared to its March forecast. "This could suggest a slight easing of monetary policy going forward," it said.

Denmark will auction inflation-linked and nominal bonds on Tuesday, while Sweden will launch a new 2036-dated bond on Wednesday.

SWITZERLAND

Swiss inflation data for May are due for release on Tuesday. Inflation eased to 0% in April, the bottom of the Swiss National Bank's 0-2% target range.

SNB Chairman Martin Schlegel recently warned that inflation could fall into negative territory. However, the SNB won't necessarily react to this scenario as its focus is on price stability over the medium term rather than the current rate of inflation, he said.

AUSTRALIA

Australian bond traders will turn their attention to the Reserve Bank of Australia's policy meeting minutes, due Tuesday.

The RBA cut rates by 25 basis points to 3.85% on May 21, with Gov. Michele Bullock revealing that the policy board discussed a deeper 50-basis-point cut amid a worsening global growth outlook.

The dovish shift marks a departure from the more balanced stance seen earlier this year, underscoring the central bank's growing concern over the economic drag from the Trump administration's trade war.

Bullock noted that while higher tariffs could fuel inflation, the broader uncertainty around global trade flows would likely have a deflationary effect. The minutes follow Bullock's recent visit to China, which highlighted the central bank's concerns about the potential impact on Australia if China's economy slows.

First-quarter gross domestic product data will be released Wednesday. While the economy has gradually regained momentum in recent quarters, overall growth remains subdued. Any indication that the economic recovery faltered in the first quarter will strengthen the case for another rate cut in July.

CHINA

Private purchasing managers' index data from China will offer insight into how tariff developments are affecting the manufacturing and services sectors, following the release of official readings.

Markets will be watching for signs of a rebound in China's sprawling manufacturing sector after activity tumbled more than expected in April as export orders cratered.

Business sentiment has taken a hit from the tariff battle, and the May prints will show if the mood has lightened after Beijing and Washington agreed to a temporary tariff reprieve.

The Caixin indexes compiled by S&P Global are due Tuesday and Thursday.

JAPAN

Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda will speak at an event in Tokyo on Tuesday, while Deputy Gov. Shinichi Uchida is scheduled to speak at the annual meeting of the Japan Society of Monetary Economics in Tokyo on Saturday.

Any remarks on the central bank's monetary-policy trajectory and quantitative-tightening plans will likely be of keen interest to investors, given recent sharp volatility in ultralong-dated JGBs.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to auction 2.6 trillion yen of 10-year sovereign debt on Tuesday and 800 billion yen of 30-year government bonds on Thursday. Of the two auctions, the latter may be more closely watched by market participants for any signs of demand by life insurers or pension funds amid large swings in ultralong-dated bond yields in recent sessions.

INDIA

India's central bank meets Friday amid market volatility, heightened risks from tariff uncertainty and continuing tensions with Pakistan.

With headline inflation at a multiyear low and tariff uncertainty posing downside risks to growth, the Reserve Bank of India has scope to keep cutting rates, Shilan Shah of Capital Economics said in a note. Any escalation in tensions in Kashmir could lead to a decline in investment and consumption, Shah added.

ANZ Research forecasts the Indian central bank to cut rates another three times by the end of 2025, as there is a need to further support growth now that inflation isn't a concern.

India, the world's fourth-largest economy, continues to face tariff risks from the U.S., including tariffs on steel and aluminum.

The RBI has signaled cautious optimism that the economy will be able to withstand headwinds posed by trade policy shifts, and analysts think India could benefit from changing supply-chain alignments.

REGIONAL ASIAN PMIs Monday sees a flood of PMI prints due for Asia, including Japan, Vietnam, Taiwan, South Korea and India, taking the pulse of manufacturing activity and sentiment in the region against the backdrop of trade tensions and uncertainty.

Economic data from Asian countries other than China is informative on the impact of the U.S.-China trade war, and some of the May data may reflect the full impact of the tariff war, ANZ said. However, PMIs from Asian economies will likely have weaker predictable power regarding China's export performance due to the limited sample size, ANZ added.

INDONESIA

Indonesia is scheduled to release its May inflation data on Monday. Inflation is expected to hold steady at 1.95%, with food inflation likely easing as prices for spices and meats declined, ANZ analysts said in a note. Softer growth and inflation trends may support two more 25-basis-point rate cuts by Bank Indonesia this year, ANZ added.

Trade data for April is also due Monday, and will show whether global tariff uncertainty has started to weigh on the country's exports.

Barclays expects a narrower trade surplus for April as imports likely bounced back from subdued levels in March.

Economists at RHB expect Indonesia's export momentum to weaken in the second quarter, and further deteriorate in the second half, as U.S. tariffs, coupled with slowing Chinese demand and softer commodity prices, pressure the economy.

SOUTH KOREA

South Korea is due to release inflation data for May on Wednesday. Consumer price increases likely eased slightly, justifying the central bank's resumption of rate cuts to support the economy.

The median forecast from a WSJ poll of seven analysts is for a 2.0% rise in the consumer-price index from a year earlier, following a 2.1% gain in April. The index is tipped to have remained flat from the prior month after edging up 0.1% in April. This comes after the Bank of Korea ended a pause in its easing cycle in May, cutting its policy rate by a quarter percentage point to 2.50%.

ANZ said in a recent note that lower retail fuel prices amid softer global oil prices have likely helped keep price growth in check in the country.

TAIWAN

Taiwan is due to report May consumer-price data on Thursday. Economists forecast the island economy's inflation at between 1.8% and 2%, compared with 2.03% in April.

Inflation has been under control and has remained around policymakers' 2% target in recent months.

Citi economists said the recent trend of service inflation cooling off but goods inflation rising continued in the month of May.

ANZ economists think Taiwan's inflation pressures are largely benign and aren't an immediate concern for policymakers. Economists broadly expect Taiwan's central bankers to leave rates unchanged at its upcoming June meeting.

SOUTHEAST ASIA CPIs

Inflation data from the Philippines and Thailand will be watched to see if price growth is in line with central-bank target ranges, as policymakers plot the path ahead in an environment of tariff uncertainty.

Analysts at Citi expect consumer prices to have fallen further in May, projecting that headline consumer-price index dropped 0.7% from the 0.2% decline in April. That would fan concerns about deflationary pressures, which could be exacerbated by an influx of cheap Chinese goods into the country as tariffs divert trade flows.

Inflation in Thailand has been below the central bank's target range of 1%-3% for two months, contracting in April for the first time in over a year. But given the lack of clarity around tariffs and continued concerns about household debt, policymakers could opt to keep rates on hold at their next meeting.

The Philippines' May print, due on Thursday, will likely go in the other direction, with Citi analysts anticipating a slight pickup in inflation to 1.6%. That would continue a trend of benign price growth and keep the case intact for more rate cuts.

The Philippine central bank said recently that it is considering further easing amid a favorable inflation outlook that supports investment and growth. It has delivered 100 basis points of rate cuts since August last year.

Write to Emese Bartha at emese.bartha@wsj.com and Jihye Lee at jihye.lee@wsj.com

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: Levi & Korsinsky, LLP Investigates Serviceaide, Inc. Data Breach
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / May 30, 2025 / Serviceaide, Inc. recently disclosed that it suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of Catholic Health patients. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to Serviceaide.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On November 15, 2024, Serviceaide, Inc. became aware of a security incident on its Catholic Health Elasticsearch database. Upon detection, Serviceaide launched an investigation with the assistance of third-party cyber security experts to determine the nature and scope of the incident. The investigation determined that an unauthorized third party gained access to sensitive data, making certain patient information from Catholic Health was publicly available between September 19 and November 5, 2024.

Serviceaide conducted a comprehensive review of the impacted data to determine what information was compromised and identified affected individuals. On March 20, 2025, Serviceaide identified persons whose sensitive data was included in the impacted data. The potentially impacted files contained personal information including name, Social Security number, date of birth, medical record number, patient account number, medical/health information, health insurance information, prescription/treatment information, clinical information, provider name, provider location, and email/username and password.

On May 9, 2025, Serviceaide filed a notice with the Vermont Attorney General and started sending out notice letters to the impacted individuals. Individuals who received notice that their personal information was compromised have rights and potential legal remedies available to them.

WHY YOU MAY BE ENTITLED TO COMPENSATION?

Data breaches are serious matters that can cause long-term damage. Hackers may use stolen information to commit identity theft, financial fraud, or other crimes. Companies that fail to secure your personal data may be held liable for the resulting harm.

HOW DO I KNOW IF I WAS AFFECTED?

If you are a Catholic Health patient and received a data breach notification letter from Serviceaide dated May 9, 2025, you are likely affected. Follow the link below to find out if you may be eligible for compensation.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1212 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.5% at $9,515.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. Metals are being pressured lower by a strengthening U.S. dollar, though a deeper sell-off is being quelled by persistent concerns of a shortage of copper ore, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. These have been fuelled by Chilean developments, as mining output threatens to disappoint while demand outlooks appear to be improving on easing trade conflict concerns, Commerzbank says. One of the most important mines in the Congo is also currently struggling with operational problems. A shortage of raw materials is being reflected in low smelting fees being charged to mine producers, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1202 ET - The latest developments in President Trump's ongoing tariff saga is reintroducing new uncertainty into the world trade situation. Trump posted on Truth Social today that China "has totally violated its agreement with us," which may require renewed tariff pressure. It's not immediately clear what China did that violated U.S. requirements to hold talks. This, along with the U.S. court system handling the case against Trump's tariffs, has injected fresh uncertainty into commodity futures. "There is a great deal of uncertainty around what the implications for commodity markets are from this week's developments, not least because we're in 'wait and see' mode while legal proceedings unfold," says Kieran Tompkins of Capital Economics in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1148 ET - CBOT grains are trading lower on the final day of trading this month. Traders are getting their books in order heading into June, says Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. "Almost everything so far today has been consolidation after the choppy week," says Setzer. "Today is month end and this is limiting fresh interest in the market." Traders anticipate trading in June to focus on weather in the U.S., which is also a reason why futures are under pressure this month. In midday trade, most-active corn futures are down 0.5%, soybeans are down 1%, and wheat is up 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1128 ET - The Fed's 5-year framework review, now underway, is expected to result in relatively narrow edits to how the Fed plans to respond to extended shortfalls in inflation. Some economists, including UT Austin's Carola Binder, pine for a deeper rethink of how the Fed steers the economy. Binder supports a shift to a nominal GDP target for the Fed--a regime where the Fed would set monetary policy not to target 2% inflation or low employment specifically, but to aim for a steady rate of nominal economic growth over time. That approach would also promote low inflation and unemployment, but would be simpler because it only includes one variable versus two, Binder argues, and it could make it easier for the Fed to explain what it's doing to the public, especially when inflation is low. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1113 ET - President Trump has taken an aggressive approach to lobbying Fed Chair Jerome Powell for lower interest rates, imploring him in speeches, private discussions and on social media to ease monetary policy. It's unusual for presidents to be so forthright, but it's not at all rare for elected officials in the U.S. and overseas to try to persuade central bankers to lower rates, University of Texas at Austin economics professor Carola Binder tells The Wall Street Journal. "Politicians want control over economic policy, and if you have a system to keep some of that control away from them, it's just designed to be contentious," Binder says. The wisdom of central-bank independence lies in removing the temptation for politicians to score quick electoral wins by meddling with rates, she says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1102 ET - Livestock futures on the CME are up in early trading, with most-active live cattle futures up 0.2% and lean hog futures up 0.9%. The USDA reported Friday that U.S. beef export sales totaled 14,700 metric tons for the week ended May 22, which is up 20% from the previous week and up 24% from the prior 4-week average. Pork sales totaled 30,500 tons, down 19% from the previous week but up 1% from the prior 4-week average. For beef, export sales are higher even as slaughter figures show lower production. "The heavier carcass weights are not enough to make up for reduced slaughter rates in overall beef production as it was in Q1," says StoneX in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1007 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are down for a third straight session as low weather-driven demand looks set to last into the first days of June while LNG feedgas flows ease and production edges up. The EIA this week reported a fifth straight triple-digit increase in inventories, and further large builds are expected before higher temperatures set in to lift demand. "As the market sees this inflection point begin to arrive-likely alongside hotter weather forecasts-Nymex gas futures may mark a bottom and pivot higher," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. Nymex natural gas is down 0.8% at $3.493/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1004 ET - Orange juice sold in retail settings falls to its lowest level since at least 2001, according to data from Nielsen. The firm says that 20.38 million gallons of orange juice were sold for the four weeks ended May 17. Meanwhile, average retail prices found a new record high for the sixth straight month, climbing to $11.27 a gallon. These trends have been sustained in recent years as Florida posts orange crops that are at their smallest since World War II, due to damage sustained from hurricanes and citrus greening disease. But FCOJ futures on the ICE have plummeted from record highs found late last year — falling back nearly 50% to $2.79 a pound today. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0915 ET - Oil and gas will be needed as energy sources and for the manufacturing of essential products for decades to come, Repsol's Chairman Antonio Brufau said Friday. Europe lacks a strategic plan for the transition away from refined fossil fuels, he said. "More than 95% of passenger and freight transport in Europe is powered by liquid fuels produced in refineries," he added. Brufau called for a reduced tax regime for renewable fuels, saying it would facilitate investment and boost consumption. Shares trade up 0.9% at 11.9 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0905 ET - Oil futures are lower with the market widely expecting OPEC+ to raise its output targets by another 411,000 barrels in July. The bearish factors that have driven the selloff this year, including tariffs and OPEC+ supply, may be mostly priced in by now, analysts at Citi say in a note. Oil has found support in "geopolitical risks vis-à-vis Iran and Russia-Ukraine, still-to-be-replenished low inventories, and firm refined product demand and cracks." The lack of significant increase in OPEC+ exports despite the accelerated unwinding of cuts has bolstered the view that the market might not turn as loose as previously expected, while Middle Eastern countries are building crude inventory for increased fuel use during the summer cooling season, they say. WTI is down 1.1% at $60.29, and Brent for August delivery is down 1.2% at $62.59 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0708 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.1% at $9,558.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. A stronger U.S. dollar is keeping downward pressure on metal prices, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Tariff uncertainty is also hitting metals demand, as a federal appeals court allowed U.S. President Trump's levies to stay in effect while the administration appeals a court decision that found them illegal. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has also said that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and may need direct contact between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, raising further concerns for metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0707 ET - Gold futures fall, but hold above the lows seen in the prior session. Futures are down 0.75% at $3,318.80 a troy ounce. The precious metal fell as low as $3,269.90/oz in volatile trading on Thursday after a U.S. court declared most tariffs imposed by President Trump were illegal, but recovered quickly on uncertainty around whether levies would be suspended immediately. An appeals court ruled that the ruling will be paused for now and tariffs would remain in effect. The trade conflict is far from over as the ruling only concerns "reciprocal" tariffs rather than sector ones, which means the economic outlook remains gloomy and interest rate cut expectations will likely remain strong, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This should benefit gold overall given the associated safe-haven demand, Commerzbank says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology, P.C. Data Breach under Investigation by Levi & Korsinsky, LLP
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / May 30, 2025 / Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology, P.C. recently disclosed that it suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of several individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On or around January 16, 2025, Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology became aware of unauthorized activity on its internal IT systems. Upon detection, Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology launched an investigation with the assistance of third-party specialists to determine the nature and scope of the incident. The investigation determined that an unauthorized third party gained access to its systems and acquired certain records.

Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology took immediate action to secure its systems and involved law enforcement. A thorough investigation and review of the impacted data concluded on March 31, 2025, confirming that personal and protected health information was included in the compromised records. The organization stated it took steps to ensure the unauthorized party deleted the stolen data to the best of its ability. The potentially impacted files contained personal information including name, address, date of birth, medical treatment information, and health insurance information, such as policy numbers.

On May 9, 2025, Marlboro-Chesterfield Pathology filed a notice with the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights and began sending out notice letters to the affected individuals. Individuals who received notice that their personal information was compromised have rights and potential legal remedies available to them.

data.title: Treasury Yields Resume Declining Path — Market Talk
data.description: Treasury yields resume their downward path after being rocked by fresh data and Trump's criticism of China's trade practices. Yields rose after the President posted that China broke its agreement with the U.S., and as April's PCE inflation moved closer to the Fed's 2% target, despite signs of resilient consumption. They kept moving up and down as the University of Michigan consumer survey showed continuing signs of gloomy sentiment and April's trade deficit shrank amid declining imports and rising exports. Yields are now falling, on path to end the week lower and the month higher. The 10-year is at 4.402% and the two-year at 3.915%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Toronto Stocks Slip; Laurentian Bank of Canada Gains on 2Q Profit
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Canadian stocks were slightly lower midday Friday.

Most sectors were declining, with the biggest losses in tech, consumer durables and energy. These were partially offset by gains in health services, communications and retail.

Canadian GDP rose by 2.2% in the first three months of the year thanks to strong goods exports even as household spending was pared back and business investment dropped in anticipation of a bruising trade war with the U.S.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell by 0.2%, to 26159.52, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 declined by 0.2%, to 1567.44.

Laurentian Bank of Canada shares rose 7.6%, to 31.01 Canadian dollars (US$22.46), after the Canadian lender reported a profit in the second quarter and lower provisions for credit losses.

Other market movers:

Shares in Canopy Growth fell sharply, by over 19%, to C$1.90, after the cannabis company reported a wider loss where analysts expected an improvement as regulatory changes in its Polish market dragged on international revenue.

MEG Energy's board of directors urged its shareholders not to take any action with regards to Strathcona Resources' unsolicited takeover bid which began Friday. Strathcona formally began its offer to acquire MEG shares at C$4.10 in cash and 0.62 a share for each MEG Energy share. MEG shares fell 1.7%, to C$24.29, while Strathcona shares rose 2%, to C$29.35.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Improved Economic Outlook Lifts Swiss Stocks
data.description:
Swiss stocks recovered on Friday as markets mull over the latest business surveys and economic data releases.

The Swiss Market Index edged up 0.33% at the end of the trading session.

Switzerland's KOF Economic Barometer climbed to 98.5 points in May from 97.1 points in April, mainly driven by positive developments in the indicator bundles for the manufacturing sector. However, the indicators related to foreign demand and private consumption are under pressure.

"After decreasing in the previous month, it remains below its medium-term average. Despite the rise in the KOF Economic Barometer, the outlook for the Swiss Economy remains subdued," the KOF Swiss Economic Institute said.

Meanwhile, the country's private sector saw its first positive improvement this year, with the KOF Business Situation Indicator rising to 10.1 in May from 9.4 in April. Business expectations for the next six months turned more optimistic, with most sectors having more favorable outlooks than before.

Over to corporates, Gurit Chief Financial Officer and acting Chief Executive Officer ad interim Javier Perez-Freije is exiting the Swiss composite materials company in November-end, with the search process for a new CFO set to begin. In the meantime, Tobias Lührig will assume his role as permanent CEO on July 1. The stock closed 1.80% lower.

Roche's fenebrutinib demonstrated low relapse rates and no disability progression in patients with relapsing multiple sclerosis for up to two years following treatment. The drugmaker's shares gained 2.04% at closing.
